reviews.author: WillBunger04
reviews.body: Best game ive ever played. played 100s of games but ive never connected to a game like this before. the story is so interesting and characters are so well written. best soundtrack ive ever listened to. the combat in the game starts off okay, but the more u learn the build crafting systems and parry timing the combat feels so smooth and fun. played and beaten the game 3 times now and still want to play more. the game has about 50 hours of content and could not recommend it more. 33/10 masterpiece.

reviews.author: unclekaggy
reviews.body: I fell in love with this game for everything it is. The graphics, the art, the play style. What people don't seem to get about clair obscur is that it is just so accessible. I have joint issues and it's one of the only games in the past 4 years that I've been able to just play without any pain. The soundtrack also can't seem to get unstuck from my head.

reviews.author: Thegreatplayer
reviews.body:
This game is so tight! This game made me feel like a kid again .it is a genuine masterpiece and its story is unforgettable. 

 I understand the gameplay might not have been for everyone, but this was my first ever turn based game and I **** enjoyed it. 

Even if you're skeptical about the turned based combat, the game is still worth playing completely for the mind blowing story and amazing world. 

This game doesn't try to make the biggest open world ever, it is decently large, but all the locations are tightly constructed and exploration is highly rewarded.  

the soundtrack alone is enough to give this game a 10/10, This game might have the greatest soundtrack oat, if not, it's at least in the top 5. 

The turned base combat never felt boring due to the addition of parrying and other mechanics such as gradient parry and jump. the animations for the powers are super tight Aswell. 

I played this on pc with an rx 7600 and a 1440p monitor on high graphics with fsr quality + fg. it gave me around 90 fps with no major stutters and only 1 crash throughout my 70 hours of playtime. perfectly playable. 

sandfall truly made a statement with this game and they deserve all the recognition and fame. 
10/10

reviews.author: Broxick
reviews.body: Beatiful combat, beatiful ost, beatiful art, beatiful story - beatiful game.

reviews.author: Yurii_A
reviews.body: The best game in 2025, although it did not deserve a nomination for "Best Role Playing Game " at The Game Awards.

reviews.author: bingbong67
reviews.body: While this game has outstanding visuals, the story telling I thought left more to be desired and Gameplay was not anything special. A solid game if you love this genre for sure, even a must play. But nothing more

reviews.author: LeviGaston
reviews.body: I have never been left as speechless by a game. I must admit, I was lucky enough to play this without knowing anything about it, except that it was a turn-based game, and that it won a lot of awards. I cannot put into words how much this game touched me, nor how fun it was. All I can say is, if you can, give it a shot. Tomorrow comes.

reviews.author: Mworld
reviews.body: Perfect game for those who like jRPGs i didn't like them though but still loved the game. Perfect story. IMO the Attack on Titan of the game industry.

reviews.author: Luzrov
reviews.body: Uno de mis juegos favoritos, sin duda. La historia es increíble, te engancha desde el principio hasta el final; el gameplay es muy, muy, muy bueno y los escenarios son impresionantes. Cada detalle está hecho con cariño, de jugadores para jugadores, y tiene un precio muy **** sí, aún necesita una mejora en la optimización; de hecho, todavía no he visto nada que la mejore con todo el tiempo que lleva ya en circulación. Pero, por lo demás, el juego está increíble. Es cine.

reviews.author: Eurogamer Germany
reviews.body: A monumental masterpiece: Despite the bugs, clunky menus, and awkward controls, its magnetic pull is simply impossible to resist.

reviews.author: TierraGamer
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is an ambitious action RPG that impresses on a technical level and through its open world design, yet it repeatedly stumbles in execution. Its early hours feel promising, and its boss encounters stand out as memorable, but excessive grinding, a narrative that loses momentum, and unclear design decisions gradually wear down the experience. Pearl Abyss demonstrates both talent and resources. However, the game attempts to cover too much without fully refining its core systems, ultimately falling short of its true potential.

reviews.author: Cultura Geek
reviews.body: An ambitious yet uneven open-world action RPG. Crimson Desert stands out for its polished visuals and an exploration system built around discovery and learning. Even so, its slow pacing, steep learning curve, and generic protagonist hold it back. It won’t be for everyone, but it still knows how to reward patience.

reviews.author: HardZone
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is an ambitious open-world RPG that successfully blends fast-paced combat, deep gameplay systems, and a vast, living world into one cohesive experience. Despite a sometimes confusing narrative and a few frustrating encounters, its depth, technical performance, and sheer amount of content make it one of the most impressive releases of the year, and a strong contender for Game of the Year.

reviews.author: KonsoliFIN
reviews.body: As an open world game Crimson Desert feels like next gen, but lame story telling makes it feel quite hollow.

reviews.author: Softpedia
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is one of those games that manages to seamlessly combine elements from our favorite RPGs and open world games. While you can see some of its original MMO roots in the quest design and side activities, it’s clearly meant to be a wonderful, immersive and engaging action RPG focused on exploration and with a good, emotional story to boot. The game features satisfying combat that rewards mastery, but which can be very punishing, especially when it comes to bosses. And it has so many great activities and side content that, along with the main game, it will take you over 200 hours to complete everything, especially if you want 100% achievements. Crimson Desert makes the most out of its massive scope and even if a few of its parts are undercooked, it’s still one of the most impressive open world experiences released in recent years.

reviews.author: GameOver.gr
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is an enormously ambitious game, both figuratively and in scope. We’ve rarely seen a single-player experience this large, in terms of content and mechanical variety. Even 100 hours in, we’re still discovering new areas, traversal methods, and bosses. However, it also proves that no matter how high the quality of the content (at least in a single-player game) diminishing returns eventually set in. In the end, a mediocre story does little to support the experience, and the initial excitement gradually gives way to a dull sense of routine.

reviews.author: Eurogamer Portugal
reviews.body: Chaotic, frustrating, but undeniably ambitious. Crimson Desert constantly tests our patience with technical problems and a confusing narrative, before letting us enjoy its ambitious combat system, exploration and stunning visuals. A game that demands time and a lot of patience for those who manage to overcome the barrier of its lack of polish.

reviews.author: Metro GameCentral
reviews.body: A bizarre mishmash of disparate gameplay elements, with absolutely no sense of coherent design or narrative… and yet its stunning game world is still a fascinating mess to explore.

reviews.author: 4P.de
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is massive and visually stunning, but to truly enjoy its game world, you’ll have to be willing to make some compromises. The controls take more than a little getting used to and can be a real pain at times, whether you’re using a gamepad or a mouse and keyboard. The quest design and gameplay guidance are among the worst I’ve experienced in a long time. The story leaves me completely cold, the characters spark zero interest, and while Crimson Desert strives for immersion with its detailed, vibrant world, high-quality animations, and many features, it repeatedly undermines that immersion itself.

reviews.author: CGMagazine
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is an ambitious open-world game that buckles under its own weight, making it a fun but flawed experience that will leave the player base divided.

reviews.author: Checkpoint Gaming
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is clearly a labour of love whose developers filled it to the brim. While many central mechanical systems work well, they are accompanied by numerous side systems that never quite earn their place in the wider world of Pywel. There is plenty to enjoy here, but you can’t help shake the feeling that if Pearl Abyss had focused on fewer systems and a more grounded narrative, Crimson Desert could have been truly special.

reviews.author: Areajugones
reviews.body: Crimson Desert demands patience and isn't afraid to challenge you, but when everything clicks, few games come close. Pywel is one of the most alive and detailed open worlds I've seen in years, the combat becomes deeply rewarding once you master it, boss fights are a genuine spectacle, and the sheer amount of things to discover and do is almost ridiculous. Pearl Abyss took its time with this one, and it shows.

reviews.author: But Why Tho?
reviews.body: This is a massive undertaking for any development team. It's ambitious to a point that it becomes curious that it was attempted. But unlike Captain Ahab, this is a level of investment and ambition that ultimately pays off in the highest order. The level of detail in Crimson Desert is unlike anything we have seen in this console generation.

reviews.author: COGconnected
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a remarkable achievement in open world design, with an inexhaustible number of things to see, do, and fight. It's a miracle that it never collapses under its own weight. It isn't perfect. By both accident and design, there are times when clarity and user-friendliness temporarily disappear, and the game's rough edges and opaque mechanics overshadow the fun. A lot of games over-promise and under-deliver. Crimson Desert is not one of them.

reviews.author: ComicBook
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a game built on impossible ambition, meticulously realized. Its UI frustrations and annoying inventory management are most certainly setbacks from true perfection, but they cannot and do not obscure the brilliance beneath. Even with these flaws, it undoubtedly stands atop the peaks of open-world games, looking down on nearly everything else alongside legends like Elden Ring. It is a triumph of scale and vision; a world that lingers long after the play session ends.

reviews.author: Combo Infinito
reviews.body: Crimson Desert delivers an ambitious open-world experience with impressive scale, strong visual impact and a combat system that becomes more engaging as it evolves. Exploration is one of its biggest strengths, offering verticality, variety and a sense of discovery that keeps the journey compelling. However, the game struggles with narrative execution, a lack of charismatic characters and several design issues, including inventory management, inconsistent boss fights and performance drops in intense moments. It’s a solid and often impressive adventure, but one that falls short of fully realizing its own potential.

reviews.author: DayOne
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is an amazing game full of wonder and frustration. I spent 110 hours completing its story and did not see half of the map, including the titular sandy-red expanse. It is an incredible achievement, living up to the promises and hype, while still needing a lot of love to get it into tip-top shape.

reviews.author: Critical Hits
reviews.body: Crimson Desert draws attention for its ambitious open-world adventure proposal, but the experience ultimately falls short of expectations. The game features a confusing narrative, with uninteresting characters and missions that feel like generic MMO tasks. The combat has many mechanics but little depth, with artificial difficulty spikes and unresponsive controls. Even with impressive graphics and a high degree of exploration freedom, issues with art direction, bugs, and design decisions make the game feel vast in content but shallow in quality.

reviews.author: Destructoid
reviews.body: Crimson Desert features a fantastic, content-rich open world where player freedom is the number one priority. There's so much to do and so many places to go to that it's easy to get lost among its ancient ruins, mystical forests, and snowy mountains. A lack of polish breeds frustration, but nothing can shake up the firm foundations of this incredible title.

reviews.author: Forbes
reviews.body: But if the question was if Crimson Desert could really be that big and really play that well and really keep you entertained the whole time, the answer, for me, is unequivocally yes.

reviews.author: DualShockers
reviews.body: Crimson Desert combines all the phenomenal aspects of some of the most notable titles of the past decades. It has the sense of discovery like Elden Ring, a deeply engaging and dense world like Skyrim, unpredictable moment-to-moment gameplay like Red Dead Redemption 2, and the exciting exploration and overall feel of the latest Zelda games.

reviews.author: Game8
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a sprawling adventure across a vast and living world that rewards every hour you put into it. The core gameplay evolves with your progress, introducing upgradable skills and increasingly demanding boss fights, with plenty of content to discover. While the narrative initially lacks direction and its threads can feel disconnected, this never outweighs the game’s many strengths. Crimson Desert is a slow burn that rewards time, patience, and curiosity.

reviews.author: Game Informer
reviews.body: [Crimson Desert is] a beautiful, exploration-rich open-world game that’s a clear technological achievement, hampered by a cornucopia of little frustrations and a stark lack of narrative depth.

reviews.author: Digital Spy
reviews.body: Crimson Desert's sandbox playground and beautifully crafted world do a lot of the heavy lifting to just about overshadow its confusing elements, generic story, and boring characters. What's here is an overwhelming amount of content and the bones of an amazing game that has to be respected for its ambitions.

reviews.author: GameGrin
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a game with a lot to offer players, but it gets held back slightly by some odd design choices that can create more of a challenge than they really need to.

reviews.author: GameSpot
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a frequently thrilling game, elevated by an open world well worth exploring and hard-hitting combat that delights with its depth and emphasis on player expression. It might not always be the most cohesive game, mixing high fantasy with steampunk and sci-fi elements, but there's nothing else quite like it, and I can't help but be impressed by how little restraint Pearl Abyss has shown in its commitment to delightful absurdity. In some respects, Crimson Desert might not be too good to be true, but it's a world worth getting lost in.

reviews.author: GAMES.CH
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a beautiful and technologically impressive collection of various gameplay and simulation ideas that struggle to find together into a cohesive whole. Over dozens of hours the game meanders, only to hit you with a moment where every obtuse element suddenly clicks together, offering a truly groundbreaking experience. Though immediately after you're tasked with something so odd, such as cleaning chimneys that you can't help but wonder if this is all an elaborate social experiment.

reviews.author: Gamer.no
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a highly ambitious open-world game. It is absolutely beautiful, packed with discovery, puzzles and combat. It doesn't always deliver on all its potential, and bugs are to be found, but in the end it is unique, mesmerizing and addicting.

reviews.author: Gamereactor UK
reviews.body: Crimson Desert can be a place where you can lose yourself for months as a player, but don't expect all those hours to be spent on an enriching adventure in a world that, I repeat, is wonderful. I can overlook certain technical issues given the scale of the project and because they may be fixed with a patch, but there are simply too many design conflicts across all the systems operating simultaneously for me to call it a 'masterpiece'. If you can simply live with that and enjoy the journey, even without necessarily understanding where it leads, then this open world is worth a try.

reviews.author: Gamekult
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is the absolute definition of a massive disappointment. It’s a Ferrari body with a lawnmower engine under the hood. Pearl Abyss tried to please everyone and ended up pleasing no one: Elden Ring fans will be driven mad by the clunky combat, Witcher fans will be bored to tears by the lack of depth, those looking for a world as organic as Breath of the Wild are in for a rude awakening, and the general public will struggle with the abysmal UI and unwarranted difficulty spikes. Riddled with technical issues unacceptable for a blockbuster of this scale, and weighed down by dated writing, Crimson Desert is on the verge of a total systemic failure. The hype train has well and truly derailed. See you in a year, after 150 patches and a mountain of DLC, which, of course, are already in the works.

reviews.author: Gamesurf
reviews.body: If your concern is that the game wasn't final yet, that's correct — there's a mountain of things still to be polished; but if you think it doesn't work or that it isn't fun, then no, I can assure you that you could spend your whole life in it listening to stories, admiring landscapes, discovering secrets, riding, saving... and even greeting people (yes, that matters too), because everyday life has never been so enjoyable, even in the small things, and the adventures that await you are truly memorable.

reviews.author: Gameliner
reviews.body: Pearl Abyss delivers a dynamic and visually stunning open world that feels alive and full of discovery. There’s plenty to do, and the game expects you to explore on your own, with challenging puzzles, memorable bosses and little handholding. The many systems and mechanics can take time to master, but are very rewarding. While the story takes a backseat and is told through lore, it is still a tale you don't want to miss. Strong performance and a great soundtrack round out this must-play experience.

reviews.author: GamesRadar+
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is messy, but as you untangle its mechanics and compensate for its flaws, elements of genius and wonder make the experience worthwhile. Take time to leave the beaten path and find out which of its many facets appeals to you, and you'll discover a game that's far better as a sandbox than as a story.

reviews.author: Gamers Heroes
reviews.body: Dark Souls has often been used to measure one's gaming prowess, but that measuring stick has changed with Crimson Desert. Not only does it boast some of the most challenging boss battles ever seen in the industry, its required levels of patience, determination, and ability have set a new gold standard.

reviews.author: Gaming Boulevard
reviews.body:
Crimson Desert is an ambitious and visually impressive
sandbox that excels in delivering exciting combat, player freedom, and a
richly interactive, immersive world. Its bold vision and emphasis on
exploration and player choice stand out as its greatest strengths,
offering a unique and experimental experience with countless ways to
approach the world. However, clunky mechanics, sometimes overwhelming
systems, and a weaker narrative hold it back from greatness. While it
may not satisfy those seeking a deep RPG or story-driven experience, it
remains a rewarding adventure for players who highly value freedom and
immersion, even if it won’t appeal to everyone.

reviews.author: GAMINGbible
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is by no means a perfect game. It’s an amalgamation of perhaps slightly too many ideas, and that makes for a weirdly paced experience that demands a lot of the player without offering much in the way of help. And yet, it left me in awe more times than anything else I’ve played in recent memory with its dazzling open-world. Crimson Desert may not be the best game you'll ever play, but it’ll certainly be up there as perhaps the most memorable.

reviews.author: JeuxActu
reviews.body: After spending over 110 hours in Pywel, there’s really no debate: Crimson Desert absolutely delivers on its promise of a massive open world. Pearl Abyss has built something huge and alive, with a real sense of freedom. You’re not constantly being guided, you have to observe, experiment, get lost and that’s honestly where the game shines. Content-wise, it’s massive. You’re looking at around 120 to 140 hours just for the main story, and easily up to 400 if you want to see everything. The combat system is also a big highlight, it’s deep, fast-paced, and pretty demanding, with tons of options. Every fight requires focus, but that’s exactly what makes it so satisfying. Visually, the game looks incredible, with a world that feels immersive and believable. That said, it’s not perfect: the difficulty can be pretty unforgiving, it takes time to really get comfortable with the controls, and the story doesn’t always hit as hard as it should. In the end, Crimson Desert is a huge game. Ambitious, sometimes tough, definitely not for everyone, but if you’re willing to fully invest yourself in it, it’s a pretty amazing experience.

reviews.author: IGN Deutschland
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is an entertaining open-world action RPG that offers a unique gameplay experience and, thanks to its vast scale, keeps you occupied for a long time, but fails to truly impress in almost every respect. In this case, less would have been more.

reviews.author: GamingTrend
reviews.body: This is a once-in-a-generation action RPG that redefines the genre, providing hundreds of hours of incredibly varied gameplay that never stops giving you new things to do. There are a handful of bugs to hammer out, but don't let that dissuade you — you're looking at your likely RPG of the year.

reviews.author:
tbd
GRYOnline.pl
reviews.body: Crimson Desert will impress some and alienate others – and both sides will have a valid point. This blend of Tears of the Kingdom, Elden Ring and Assassin’s Creed continues to intrigue with its open-world design and interactivity, but I fear that long before I reach the end of the story, I’ll grow weary of its sheer scale and the constant struggle with the poorly designed UI and inventory system. [Review in Progress]

reviews.author: Hardcore Gamer
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is one of a kind. It’s a spectacular adventure that has a phenomenally crafted open world to explore and an immense number of systems at play.

reviews.author: IGN France
reviews.body: Although there are still a few areas that could use some tweaking —particularly in terms of ergonomics— Crimson Desert manages to live up to the hype. It’s not perfect, but the authenticity of its world, the richness of its content, and its engaging gameplay make it a standout title for the start of 2026. Pearl Abyss has a diamond that’s still a bit rough around the edges, but with a little polish, it’ll be nothing short of divine.

reviews.author: Insider Gaming
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a game of extremes. At its best, it offers a breathtaking world filled with meaningful exploration, impressive scale, and genuinely thrilling battles. However, that brilliance is occasionally weighed down by an overabundance of unfocused systems, a weak narrative, and combat that struggles to maintain consistency.

reviews.author: IGN Adria
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a visually impressive and highly ambitious open-world RPG with thrilling, adrenaline-fueled combat. While its complexity can occasionally feel more like a drawback than a strength, for fans of RPGs and open-world games, this is a title that always has something more to offer.

reviews.author: IGN Benelux
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is an excellent game with an engaging open world, highly challenging boss fights, and mind-bending puzzles. The game is certainly not perfect, but Pearl Abyss' willingness to listen to feedback gives me hope that Crimson Desert will only continue to improve in the future on what is already a strong foundation.

reviews.author: IGN Brasil
reviews.body: Crimson Desert offers a well-rounded experience, even if it has a few stumbles along the way. Its campaign leaves something to be desired, but the game more than makes up for it with a vibrant, detail-rich world. Its excellent combat, combined with stunning visuals and a powerful soundtrack, makes the battles fun and epic, but it was in its open world that I found the game’s greatest appeal and standout feature.

reviews.author: Loot Level Chill
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is frustrating and buggy and can be very player-unfriendly, but it's also beautiful, deep, seemingly endless and monumentally, perhaps detrimentally, ambitious.

reviews.author: Screen Rant
reviews.body: You have to admire its ambition, and there are high points, but there's also a lot of slogging around and meaningless fetch quests.

reviews.author: MGG
reviews.body: Crimson Desert’s best moments come when you think about the game it could have been, were it not for its overabundance of clunky mechanics and its painfully sluggish controls. Riddled with glitches and systems that don’t work, Pearl Abyss’s first single-player game can rely on its sublime open world, or its core management system borrowed from BDO, to occasionally lift us out of our frustration.

reviews.body: Jogo maravilhoso, claro que tem muita coisa a melhorar mas muito bom. Jogo de se perder, muito bom em desafios. Achei a história principal fraca porém não atrapalha. Muito bom! QI de 80 necessário pra jogar.

reviews.body: The game is amazing and beautiful! I'm playing it on PS5 Pro. The graphics and the RT are just mind blowing! The are few bug, but Pearl Abiss gained my trust since the launch day. Patch notes must keep on coming to solve some issues, but **** game is incredible!!! Loving it!

reviews.author: nickybyloo
reviews.body: The perfect game for me. Not a perfect game for many, I'm sure. It is a slow burn game that doesn't force you to rush and speedrun its progression, because realistically, there is just so much to see and do that it's not even part of your story points of interest. The very small and even larger details are so wonderful to behold and experience but just wondering around looking and discovering, even just within a very small space in your immediate area. This world is busy and full of life. The story is interesting enough but not the motivating factor, rather it is just the means by how the game onboards you to the world and its systems and mechanics. I love Crimson Desert. I think I will be playing this for thousands of hours.

reviews.author: SirMustachio
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is technical, deep, beautiful, challenging and extremely satisfying for those who truly play it. If someone gave it a bad rating because "it's difficult" or "I didn't understand it," that's not a problem with the game, it's a skill issue.

reviews.author: DanEsmGaming
reviews.body: I’ll likely adjust this review down to a 9 over time, but so far, I am absolutely digging this game. 15 hours in so far. If you like open world sandbox games with tons of content (think Skyrim and Breath of the Wild) you will like this game.

reviews.author: rexiopa
reviews.body: wide as the ocean deep as a puddle, is not something i imagined i would describe this game when i first played it. i've been following its news throughout the years since announcement which was way before the hype wave started the month before release, so my expectations weren't particularly that insanely high as they were slowly tempered with each blog post and news i could find teasing what the game is actually like. so now the game is out what can i say about it? it's nothing short of a lifeless, tasteless and utterly unrewarding game that doesn't respect my time which for a massive sandbox is ironic i know except this game has a story progression lock on most things you're gonna want to use to play the game. it'll force you to suffer through its terrible story filled with hours and hours of endless unskippable cutscenes and dialogue just so that you'd realize in the end that you don't care for any of those characters. i swear sometimes i just stop while doing a quest and ask just why the hell am i doing any of this? which is a common feeling you'll get as this game doesn't explain much about the stuff you're told to do as you're just meant to do it. gameplay is the more forgiving part of the game and for the most part the game lets you wander off and take in the world which is a marvel to look at and much less to explore, you probably heard the exploration is the best part of the game but so much of the exploration ties back to quest completion and a really weird insistence on luring you into those places of interest by highlighting them on the map for you something that we would absolutely burn games for doing so if this game was made by say Ubisoft. i don't feel rewarded by having my hand held while i'm out there exploring, i don't even feel rewarded accordingly when i put in the effort to solve puzzles and happen upon random stuff since the loot is so bad. while we're talking about gameplay i'd like to turn your attention to something sandbox games are famous for, which is freedom in playing and completing stuff the way you like and that my friend is something sorely missing in this game. you're not allowed to go off script by at all, in fact you'd be punished for wandering off far enough that the game would reset your quest and berate you for not following the script exactly as they tell you to do it. i'm not asking here for different story outcomes even though the game seems to tease you can do that but with absolutely no consequence, i am asking however for variety of approach to complete the same quest and reach the same outcome. that doesn't exist here at all and the game has that for all quests so far, they all feel samey and formulaic the way Skyrim used to do those radiant quests and it's a complete turn off for what could have been a truly remarkable game. there's so much else i can complain about and nitpick about it but usually with these sort of games i would easily overlook this for what the game does that's really fun. i'm struggling to find something like that here, the closest thing that keeps me coming back to try it out and find some hope for it is the fact this world is massively ambitious, incredibly gorgeous and deeply interactive, this isn't to contradict my first point about being deep as a puddle since the game's NPCs are brainless idiots with no lives to speak of, they don't exist outside of you and your decisions which takes you out of the immersion immediately once you notice it and it really screws with me.

The combat in this game is incredible, every weapon feels unique, the powers you can imbue them with feel amazing, and the variety of enemies is impressive.

reviews.body: The game defo has some minor issues, but what this game achieved in term of open world innovation is absolutely amazing, it’s not even about the lack of what to do, it’s about what should i do first, the amount of content is gigantic, the world is beautiful, the combat is skillful, the bosses are absolute aura farmers, the drips are 10s and more… i love this game and i still have only scratched the surface after 40h of playtime, can’t wait to play more.

reviews.author: ChaosTheory21
reviews.body: I suspect the pro reviewers encountere bugs and rushed the game to completion because I absolutely love the world of Crimson Desert. The music is beautiful , the graphics special, and the game is massive. Sure there’s some laughable recreations of elements from TOTK and RDR2, but there’s enough in CD that is innovative to make it stand on its own. Take the game slow and it feels like you’re entering the worlds of Skyrim or Elden Ring for the first time again. Can’t wait to get lost in all it has to offer!

reviews.author: Bedirhan_77
reviews.body: I'm almost at 40 hours and the game continues to surprise me with every hour. I haven't been able to focus on it due to work, but I'm constantly excited to play it again. I didn't find the story bad; at least it's followable. It reminds me of Assassin's Creed Shadow because of its open world (Crimson Desert is far better), its quest system (Shadow is absolutely terrible), and similar features. I couldn't finish Shadow, but when playing Crimson Desert, instead of getting bored, I keep playing wondering what new things I'll see next. It's not for everyone, yes, but once you get hooked, you don't even realize how time flies.

reviews.author: SilentKnight88
reviews.body:
Crimson Desert is one of those rare games that completely dissolves the concept of time. I sit down intending to play for 30 minutes, maybe an hour… and suddenly three hours have vanished. As someone with a family and real responsibilities, that’s both incredible and mildly terrifying. But it speaks to the game’s greatest strength: it’s effortlessly immersive.

I’m about 15 hours in, and every minute has been a blast. The world feels alive, the pacing is tight, and the game constantly rewards curiosity. Whether I’m exploring, fighting, or just wandering into some unexpected side encounter, Crimson Desert nails that open‑world “one more thing” loop better than almost anything I’ve played in years.

What surprises me most is how far off the professional reviews feel. Critics framed this game as messy or unfocused, but that hasn’t been my experience at all. If anything, the game’s variety is one of its biggest strengths. It’s ambitious, yes, but it’s also cohesive, polished, and confident in what it wants to be. The disconnect between critic scores and player experience here is huge, and honestly, it’s **** me, Crimson Desert is easily a 9 at minimum, and to me, it’s a 10. 

It checks every box I want from an open‑world action‑adventure game: 

Combat that feels weighty, responsive, and fun… A world that begs to be explored… A steady stream of meaningful content… Visuals that are consistently jaw‑dropping… A gameplay loop that hooks you instantly and refuses to let go…

Most importantly, the game respects your time while somehow stealing all of it. That’s a rare magic trick. Crimson Desert is special and years from now it will be named among the great games of this generation. And if the early critic reviews scared anyone off, they’re missing out on one of the most engaging open‑world experiences in years.

reviews.author: Sam_Agiota
reviews.body: Crimson desert is a game that starts off confusing, complex, and not very accessible. In the first three hours, there are too many buttons, too many controls, too many commands. But after the first five hours, you begin to feel immersed in a vast, beautiful, and highly dynamic world that provides hours and hours of fun. I’m currently 50 hours in and enjoying it a lot. I recommend it to everyone, and don’t believe the 4.5 ratings given to this game. It’s an exceptional game — a masterpiece.

reviews.author: JF11
reviews.body: Crimson Desert is a visually stunning game easily one of the best-looking titles of its generation, with incredible environments that truly stand out. In terms of gameplay and mechanics, the sheer variety of options, systems, and content is remarkable, the game feels vast and full of things to do. The story isn’t its strongest point, but honestly, with visuals and open-world exploration like this, it doesn’t feel that important. Not every game needs to focus heavily on narrative, and in this case, the clear emphasis is on gameplay. Overall, I’d give it a 9/10. The only real improvement would be a stronger story otherwise, it’s a fantastic game.
